title: Ship It Before Midnight
tagline: A production outage, a release everyone's watching, and a rollback button that isn't a free action.

wake_up.text: Today's the day the quarter's biggest feature ships. Slack already has forty messages in the release channel before you've had coffee.

choices.text: Read through the entire release thread before responding to anything
Skim for anything urgent and get moving

standup.text: Standup: the release is scheduled for 2 PM. QA flagged one edge case yesterday that got marked "won't fix for this release."

choices.text: Push back and ask the team to reconsider the edge case
Accept the team's call and move forward

code_review.text: A teammate's pull request needs review before the release branch can be cut. The diff is large.

choices.text: Do a genuinely thorough review, line by line
Skim it and approve — you trust them

random_moment.text: The morning fills in around the review, the way any office morning does — a little differently every day.

choices.text: A teammate does a coffee run for the whole floor
Slack goes down for twenty minutes at the worst time
Someone puts on a genuinely great office playlist

deploy_prep.text: Pre-deploy checklist: migrations staged, feature flags configured, rollback plan documented — in theory.

choices.text: Actually dry-run the rollback plan before deploying
Trust the documented plan without testing it

lunch_break.text: An hour until the scheduled deploy window. Half the team is already glued to their screens instead of eating.

choices.text: Actually step away and eat
Eat at your desk while double-checking the release notes

deploy_start.text: The deploy kicks off. Dashboards up on every screen, the whole team watching error rates tick across in real time.

choices.text: Monitor the rollout

production_outage.text: Twelve minutes in, error rates spike hard — the edge case from yesterday's standup, live in production, taking checkout down for a slice of users and climbing.

choices.text: Roll back the deploy immediately
Push a hotfix forward instead of rolling back
Page the whole on-call rotation before deciding anything
Monitor closely and wait to see if it's self-limiting

outage_outcome_rollback.text: The rollback executes clean, exactly as dry-run tested it this morning. Error rates flatten within ninety seconds. The dry run this morning just paid for itself.

choices.text: Start the retro on what happened

outage_outcome_hotfix.text: Under real pressure, the fix goes out fast and it's correct — a genuinely impressive read of the problem under a ticking clock. It's also a risk that could have gone the other way.

choices.text: Start the retro on what happened

outage_outcome_page.text: Half the engineering org is now staring at the same dashboard. The rollback happens a few minutes later than it needed to, with several more opinions in the room than the decision required.

choices.text: Start the retro on what happened

outage_outcome_wait.text: It wasn't self-limiting. Eighteen minutes of degraded checkout before the rollback finally goes out — eighteen minutes that didn't need to happen.

choices.text: Start the retro on what happened

postmortem.text: A blameless postmortem doc needs writing while everything's still fresh — the temptation to soften the timeline is real.

choices.text: Write the full, honest timeline, including your own calls
Soften the rougher parts of the timeline

afternoon_recovery.text: The rest of the team is rattled. Someone needs to decide what happens with the release for tomorrow.

choices.text: Check in with the team before touching the release plan
Get straight into replanning tomorrow's release

evening_fix.text: The actual fix for the edge case needs writing properly now, not as a rushed hotfix — tests included this time.

choices.text: Write the tests first, then the fix
Ship the fix and backfill tests tomorrow

end_shift.text: The dashboards are green again. Tomorrow's release will carry the weight of today whether anyone says so out loud or not.

choices.text: Close the laptop

triumphant.title: Incident Commander
triumphant.blurb: The outage was real, and the response was clean — fast, calm, honestly documented. This is the incident people cite when they explain why they trust you on-call.

steady_hand.title: Solid Response
steady_hand.blurb: No heroics, no panic. Just careful engineering under real pressure, start to finish.

ordinary_day.title: Shipped, Eventually
ordinary_day.blurb: A release day with a real bump in it, handled, closed out. Tomorrow's release is already on the calendar.

burned_out.title: Running on Fumes
burned_out.blurb: The dashboards are green, but you're not sure you are. This pace, incident after incident, has a real cost.

written_up.title: Postmortem Follow-Up
written_up.blurb: Something about today's incident response is going in front of engineering leadership. The outage is resolved. The process still needs answers.
